FAQs

1. What is the Molecular Sciences Institute?

The Molecular Sciences Institute is an independent, nonprofit research laboratory that combines genomic experimentation with computer modeling. The mission of MSI is to predict the behavior of cells and organisms in response to defined genetic and environmental changes. Progress toward this goal will significantly increase our understanding of biological systems and help catalyze radical changes in how diseases are understood and treated.

2. Are there other organizations conducting similar kinds of research?

MSI is the only institution that was established specifically to explore the emerging field of predictive biology. As an independent, nonprofit research laboratory that operates free of commercial or academic pressures, MSI scientists are free to pursue groundbreaking, fundamental biology research in an interdisciplinary, collaborative environment without needing to focus on near-term commercial applications of their work. As predictive biology pervades basic research, we believe that many universities and other institutes will pursue similar lines of work with similar goals, although their approaches may vary. We look forward to working with additional collaborators in the years ahead.

3. What makes MSI unique?

The MSI's approach and methods towards answering important biological problems makes it a unique organization. Established in 1996, MSI has led the effort to make the multidisciplinary pursuit of important biological problems of the 21st century a widespread practice.

4. What is open source biology?

MSI is committed to making its research and its technology available to benefit the public. To this end, MSI publishes its scientific results in the open literature, makes reagents and methods freely available to the research community, and posts unpublished data on the web. MSI also distributes its software under established Open Source licensing arrangements.

Researchers at the MSI have been working with other institutions, scientists, engineers, and legal experts to develop the concept of "Open Source Biology". If viable "Open Source" licensing schemes for biological methods and reagents can be developed, the Institute intends to use these schemes, in combination with existing schemes or even exclusively, to satisfy the criterion that the new technologies are disseminated for maximum public benefit.

How will this affect the commercialization of your work?

Keeping biology "open source" does not preclude commercialization. The open source biology concept is to make sure that biological information, materials, and methods are widely available. In some cases, public benefit may be better served by commercial development of particular technologies to speed innovation and dissemination.

5. What is the Alpha Project?

The Alpha Project is an ambitious effort to examine communication within cells and to develop computer models that accurately predict intra-cellular signaling. One ultimate goal of this research is to reveal insights that enable much more precisely targeted treatments for diseases. The Alpha Project's focus is on the flow and processing of information within cells in a prototypical pathway, the pheromone signal transduction pathway in a well studied, single cell organism, baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae). Though a simple organism, yeast cells have much in common with cells of more complex organisms, including humans. By developing and applying innovative experimental and computational approaches to this system, MSI scientists will generate an interactive model of the pathway. The Alpha Project, the research methods and the computer models developed in the course of the work are a pilot study to explore similar pathways in higher organisms.

6. Why are you using yeast cells and how are they similar to human cells?

Yeast was chosen for study because it is a simple, single celled organism and is one of the most well studied in biology. Its genome has been sequenced, its genes have been identified, it has decades of physiological data attached to it, and it is easy to manipulate in the lab. Higher organisms such as humans contain cells with molecular components that are involved in signal processing pathways. These components are very similar to those in yeast cells, so studying genes of this simple cell could have vast implications for studying how genes work in more complex organisms.

7. How will the end-outcome of the Alpha project benefit the general public?

Success in the Alpha Project will result in the creation of advanced tools that basic and biomedical researchers can use to understand and predict cellular behavior. This ability will go a long way towards understanding diseases, helping researchers develop precise chemical treatments and knowing what effect those treatments will have on humans, animals, and plants.

8. How is this different from the Human Genome Project?

This work builds on the success of the Human Genome Project. The Human Genome Project has shown us what proteins are encoded by the genome, but we still don't know very much about how individual proteins within cells interact with each other to cause diseases or other complex outcomes. The MSI's work aims to understand this choreography so that we can predict the results of cellular changes, and ultimately, how certain changes contribute to disease.

Though the Human Genome Project actually began as an international research effort to determine the DNA sequence of the human genome and the genomes of other organisms used extensively in biological research, knowing the DNA sequence is only part of the answer. The next step is to understand function, which results from the interaction of genomes with their environment. The goals of the Human Genome Project were therefore expanded in 1998 to develop new and improved research tools, approaches, and capabilities necessary to understand and use genomic DNA sequence. Centers of Excellence, such as the Center at the MSI, are being established to pursue advanced genome research as part of the expanded goals of the Human Genome Project.

9. How is this similar to proteomics?

Proteomics is a term used for systematizable research aimed at understanding proteins, the functional products of DNA and the actual workhorses of the cell. The work of the Alpha Project is aimed at examining all the proteins involved in a particular cellular function . Many of the techniques developed during the Alpha Project will be used by proteomic researchers.

10. How is the MSI funded?

MSI is primarily supported by federal grants and from other funds provided by foundations and corporations.
